title: List EVERY available RAM config per GPU as its own picker row (e.g. RTX 5090 Â· 60GB and RTX 5090 Â· 90GB as two rows) â€” spike whether RunPod's API can surface per-host configs at all
description:
GOAL (user, 2026-07-01 â€” SUPERSEDES the old 'wait-for/detect high-RAM' framing): the RunPod GPU picker in Settings should list EACH available host config as its OWN row, showing RAM as well as VRAM per row. Example: if EU-RO-1 has an RTX 5090 host with 60GB system RAM AND another RTX 5090 host with 90GB system RAM (same $/hr â€” RunPod price is fixed per card, independent of RAM config or DC), the picker shows BOTH as separate selectable rows so the user picks the RAM config they want (LTX warm perf needs ~90GB; a 60GB host thrashes â†’ weights re-fault every gen â†’ ~2:14 vs ~22-25s). Today the picker shows ONE row per GPU type with a single collapsed RAM number.

WHY THE OLD FRAMING WAS DROPPED: prior versions of this card chased levers A (post-connect detect+warn), B (wait-for-high-RAM poll), C (picker hint). User's real want is simpler and better: just SHOW every config and let them pick. B/C only make sense if the picker can't enumerate configs; if it CAN, direct selection beats poll-and-hope.

HARD BLOCKER (investigated 2026-07-01, read-only sweep of routes/runpodRemote.js + remoteProxy.js): the app makes only TWO RunPod GraphQL queries and NEITHER exposes per-host configs â€” both COLLAPSE all hosts into one number:
  1. `gpuTypes { ... lowestPrice(input:{gpuCount,dataCenterId,secureCloud}){ minMemory minVcpu } }` (runpodRemote.js ~line 130) â†’ `lowestPrice` is a SINGLE OBJECT, one scalar `minMemory` = the cheapest offering's RAM floor. NOT a list. No `perHostConfigs` array.
  2. `dataCenters { ... gpuAvailability { available gpuTypeId stockStatus } }` (runpodRemote.js ~line 141) â†’ one `available` bool + `stockStatus` string per GPU per DC. NO RAM, no host count, no config breakdown.
So the picker builds exactly ONE row per GPU type (MpiSettings.js _buildGpuOptions ~line 1408), RAM = the single `minMemory` scalar. RunPod's scheduler picks the physical host OPAQUELY at deploy â€” the app never sees the 60GB-vs-90GB split. AND the deploy calls (REST POST /pods remoteProxy.js:603; GraphQL podFindAndDeployOnDemand runpodRemote.js:174) send NO min-RAM constraint â€” no `minMemoryInGb` field anywhere.

WHY IT'S NOT AUTOMATICALLY DEAD: RunPod has DISABLED GraphQL introspection (MPI-77 brief). The app only knows fields that were empirically PROBED â€” the full schema is hidden. So two undocumented capabilities MIGHT exist and can only be confirmed by a LIVE PROBE against the real API (introspection off = can't enumerate; must test candidate fields against a real key):
  (i) A host-listing / offerings query returning configs-per-GPU WITH their RAM. If it exists â†’ picker fans out into real per-config rows. THIS is the whole feature.
  (ii) A `minMemoryInGb` (or similar) input on podFindAndDeployOnDemand / POST pods. If it exists â†’ even without a listing query, synthesize RAM-tier rows (60/90) and each deploys with its own RAM floor; RunPod places accordingly.
Either one unlocks the feature. Neither is knowable from the codebase.

STAGE 1 â€” LIVE API PROBE (GATE; do FIRST, decides everything): with a real RunPod API key against EU-RO-1 (where the user SAW both 60GB and 90GB 5090 hosts), (a) test candidate host-listing/offerings GraphQL queries for a per-config RAM list; (b) probe podFindAndDeployOnDemand's input for an accepted `minMemoryInGb`/min-RAM field (send it, see if the mutation accepts vs schema-400s). Write a throwaway probe script (needs live key + willingness to burn a test deploy or two). Save findings to research/.

STAGE 2 â€” BUILD (branch on probe result):
  - Listing query exists â†’ fan the picker into per-config rows in MpiSettings.js _buildGpuOptions; each row carries its own RAM + VRAM + (same) price; deploy targets that config.
  - Only `minMemoryInGb` works â†’ synthesize RAM-tier rows (e.g. 60GB / 90GB), deploy each with its RAM-floor constraint added to the create spec (remoteProxy.js:603 + runpodRemote.js:174 input).
  - NEITHER works â†’ feature is IMPOSSIBLE with RunPod's API; document the dead end in research/, and fall back to the old lightweight levers (post-connect RAM warn via /wrapper/stats when under ~90GB; optional wait-for-high-RAM poll on lowestPrice.minMemory since price is flat = free to wait). Close or re-scope accordingly.

FILES: routes/runpodRemote.js (gpuTypes/lowestPrice ~116-146, podFindAndDeployOnDemand ~174-192), routes/remoteProxy.js (POST /pods spec ~603-652), js/components/Compounds/LandingPages/MpiSettings/MpiSettings.js (_buildGpuOptions ~1357-1435 â€” the row builder). Introspection-off note + confirmed-working args live in _archived/MPI-77/brief.md; lowestPrice per-DC discrepancy in docs/gotchas.md RunPod section.

STAGE 1 PROBE DONE 2026-07-01 (live API, read-only, no pod â€” research/api-probe-2026-07-01.md): (1) NO host-listing query exists â€” `gpuTypes.lowestPrice` for the 5090 is ONE collapsed scalar (minMemory:60), the extra host-count fields (rentedCount/totalCount/rentalPercentage) are schema-valid but return null, `gpuAvailability` is one row per GPU/DC (no RAM), and `availableGpuTypes` does not exist. So 'list every RAM config as its own row' is IMPOSSIBLE as literally stated â€” the API has no per-config enumeration. (2) BUT `minMemoryInGb` IS an accepted input on podFindAndDeployOnDemand (passed schema validation; failed only on bogus GPU, not unknown-field). Whether it STEERS placement is UNPROVEN â€” all placement failures collapse to a generic SUPPLY_CONSTRAINT error, so the only proof is a REAL deploy (minMemoryInGb:90 on an in-stock 5090 â†’ read landed pod's actual RAM via /wrapper/stats, vs a control run without the field). Costs a brief deletable pod.

REFRAMED GOAL (post-probe): NOT per-config rows. Instead â€” IF the live deploy proves `minMemoryInGb` steers placement: add RAM-TIER picker rows (e.g. 'RTX 5090 Â· â‰¥90 GB (LTX)' vs 'any RAM', same price) and pass minMemoryInGb into the create spec (remoteProxy.js POST /pods spec + the GraphQL fallback input runpodRemote.js:174). Deterministic high-RAM landing = better than the old poll lever. IF it's ignored: fall back to lever A (post-connect RAM warn via /wrapper/stats under ~90GB) + optional lever B (wait-for-high-RAM poll on lowestPrice.minMemory; flat price = free to wait).

LIVE DEPLOY PROOF DONE 2026-07-01 (4 real ephemeral 5090s in EU-RO-1, each created+RAM-read+deleted; orphan sweep confirmed reaped:[]): minMemoryInGb IS HONORED as a hard placement FLOOR. baseline/16/90 all created (landed 92GB); minMemoryInGb:200 FAILED with SUPPLY_CONSTRAINT â€” only the RAM value varied â†’ RunPod filters placement by it. FEASIBLE, proven. Full table in research/api-probe-2026-07-01.md ADDENDUM.

FINAL SHAPE (answers the user's 'two entries?' question): NOT a live 60-vs-90 enumeration (API can't list configs). Instead the filter is a FLOOR â€” so ship ONE meaningful extra picker row: a HIGH-RAM 'LTX' variant, e.g. 'RTX 5090 Â· â‰¥90 GB (LTX)'. Selecting it passes minMemoryInGb:90 â†’ guaranteed â‰¥90GB host or a clean SUPPLY_CONSTRAINT (pairs with the existing wait-for-stock toggle; flat price = free to wait). A '~60GB' row is NOT worth building â€” a 60-floor still accepts a 90GB host, so it can't guarantee low, and nobody wants low. The value is guaranteeing HIGH for LTX. So: baseline row (any RAM) + one 'â‰¥90GB LTX' row per LTX-relevant GPU.

BUILD (recommended, research doc has exact lines): add minMemoryInGb to the create spec in BOTH paths â€” REST POST /pods (remoteProxy.js ~603) + GraphQL fallback input (runpodRemote.js createPodGraphql ~175: `if (typeof spec.minMemoryInGb==='number') input.minMemoryInGb=spec.minMemoryInGb;`). Add the 'â‰¥90GB LTX' variant to the picker (_buildGpuOptions MpiSettings.js ~1408), thread the constraint through the connect flow, pair with wait-for-stock. PRE-SHIP CHECK: only the GraphQL create path was proven live â€” quick-probe that REST POST /pods ALSO accepts minMemoryInGb (or route high-RAM creates through the GraphQL path).

BRANCH: RunPod. Research spike COMPLETE + FEASIBLE. Ready to build when scheduled.
